facts.text: Rome was founded along an ancient Native American portage path known as the Oneida Carrying Place, making it a historic crossroads of the Iroquois.
In 1851, Jesse Williams founded the first cheese factory in the United States right in Rome, sparking a regional dairy boom.
The Sears Oil Company station, built in 1930, was restored in 2002 and now serves as a museum of early automotive history.
Rome’s historic Martin Street Train Station, still served by Amtrak, offers daily departures and connects the city to the national rail network.
The city developed at the site of Fort Stanwix, a 1758 British fort that later became a National Monument after the 1935 Fort Stanwix Act.
Rome’s Erie Canal Village, established in the 1840s, preserves canal-era life and shows how the canal reshaped New York’s economy.
